## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the current diff and apply the fixes — equivalent to /code-review --fix.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

  Generate the rbtd fixture-name consts from their canonical bash owner via the shared primitive and migrate consumers off the hand-mirror in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`.
  
  ## Shape
  
  - Fixture names are canonically authored in `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh` and hand-copied into `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`. Single home exists — projectable without a tidy step.

12	- **Constant-projection scope: single canonical bash author, or out.** A constant is projectable to another language only if exactly one bash module canonically owns it. Constants whose identity is distributed across bash, disk, and Rust are excluded unless a separate canonical-author decision is taken first.
13	- **One generated landing file, one minted prefix `RBTDGC_`, fed per-pair.** All five co-maintained groups (colophons, RBCC set, fixtures, operation verbs, container roles) project into a single generated Rust file in the rbtd crate — new file, not new crate — under the `RBTDGC_` prefix. The consts never carry a bash module prefix (`RBZ_`, `RBCC_`, … are bash-only namespaces; stamping them on a Rust const is a false ownership claim). The cross-language correspondence is mechanical and fully generated: Rust const = `RBTDGC_` + the bash const's stem (its bash-module prefix stripped, stem carried verbatim) — one uniform rule, no per-entry mapping, hence no drift. A shared RBK-ignorant BUK primitive formats one `pub const` line; each group's canonical bash home loops its own data through it (bash 3.2 has no clean way to pass a pair-list to a function, but per-pair scalar calls are trivial). The legacy curated names (`RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*`, the lib.rs path macros, etc.) carry no preservation obligation — each is retired as its group's generated consts land.

Groups with a single bash home are projected directly: colophons (rbz), the RBCC tinder set incl. credential roles and .env filenames (rbcc), fixture names (rbte). Groups scattered across bash today — operation verbs and container roles — earned a canonical-author tidy first (one bash home in rbcc), then project. This honors the single-author rule rather than weakening it.

# jjezs_paddock ₣BP

## Shape

Grab-bag cleanup heat. Theme: consolidate to the single rightful owner — remove capability and constants that are redundantly held in more than one place. Collects post-cutover RBK cleanups of that nature.

The constant-projection thread is the bulk of the heat. A survey found the co-maintained set is broader than colophons alone — colophons, credential roles, fixture names, .env filenames, operation verbs, and container roles all live in bash and are hand-copied into the rbtd Rust manifest or lib.rs, kept in sync only by a runtime drift check. The work points every co-maintained group through bash→Rust generation, retiring the runtime drift check in favor of a build-time diff gate.

## Locked decisions

- **Work that reads RBCC or the moorings layout is gated on ₣BK (moorings cutover) closing** — stable RBCC, settled layout, RBBC aliases retired. Cleanups touching neither are free to run.
- **Constant-projection scope: single canonical bash author, or out.** A constant is projectable to another language only if exactly one bash module canonically owns it. Constants whose identity is distributed across bash, disk, and Rust are excluded unless a separate canonical-author decision is taken first.
- **One generated landing file, one minted prefix `RBTDGC_`, fed per-pair.** All five co-maintained groups (colophons, RBCC set, fixtures, operation verbs, container roles) project into a single generated Rust file in the rbtd crate — new file, not new crate — under the `RBTDGC_` prefix. The consts never carry a bash module prefix (`RBZ_`, `RBCC_`, … are bash-only namespaces; stamping them on a Rust const is a false ownership claim). The cross-language correspondence is mechanical and fully generated: Rust const = `RBTDGC_` + the bash const's stem (its bash-module prefix stripped, stem carried verbatim) — one uniform rule, no per-entry mapping, hence no drift. A shared RBK-ignorant BUK primitive formats one `pub const` line; each group's canonical bash home loops its own data through it (bash 3.2 has no clean way to pass a pair-list to a function, but per-pair scalar calls are trivial). The legacy curated names (`RBTDRM_COLOPHON_*`, the lib.rs path macros, etc.) carry no preservation obligation — each is retired as its group's generated consts land.
- **Full co-maintained set is in scope** — not just colophons. Groups with a single bash home are projected directly: colophons (rbz), the RBCC tinder set incl. credential roles and .env filenames (rbcc), fixture names (rbte). Groups scattered across bash today — operation verbs and container roles — earned a canonical-author tidy first (one bash home in rbcc), then project. This honors the single-author rule rather than weakening it.

## What done looks like

The redundancies in scope are gone: no capability that has a better-owned home elsewhere survives in two places, and no co-maintained constant group is hand-maintained on both sides of the bash↔Rust boundary. The `RBTDGC_` generated file feeds the rbtd manifest's former hand-mirror groups and the lib.rs path macros; the runtime drift check is retired in favor of a build-time diff gate (the same fresh-generate-and-diff staleness check the qualifier already applies to the generated markdown context).

# jjezs_pace ₢BPAAG

## Character

Mechanical projection over a single-homed group; mirrors the colophon shape against a different bash owner.

## Goal

Generate the rbtd fixture-name consts from their canonical bash owner via the shared primitive and migrate consumers off the hand-mirror in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`.

## Shape

- Fixture names are canonically authored in `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh` and hand-copied into `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`. Single home exists — projectable without a tidy step.
- Feed the names through the shared primitive into the `RBTDGC_` file (const name `RBTDGC_` + fixture stem); migrate manifest consumers and any inline fixture literals onto the generated consts.
- Keep the runtime drift check alive until the diff-gate lands.

## Sources

- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh` — fixture-name canonical author
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — fixture hand-mirror

## What done looks like

Fixture consts are generated `RBTDGC_*`, not hand-written; consumers reference the generated names; Rust builds and the fast suite is green.

108	# Rust const projection (rbcc single-homed set → RBTDGC_)
109	
110	# rbcc_emit_consts() - Emit the RBCC-owned co-maintained constants as Rust
111	# string consts to stdout, one `pub const` line per name/value pair via the
112	# shared buz_emit_const primitive (BUK must be kindled). The single-homed set:
113	# moorings/vessels dirs, credential roles, .env filenames. Each Rust const is
114	# RBTDGC_ + the RBCC stem (RBCC_ prefix stripped) uppercased; the value is
115	# carried verbatim. Bash stays mixed-case (RBCC_moorings_dir); the generated
116	# Rust is SCREAMING (RBTDGC_MOORINGS_DIR) per Rust convention — that casing is
117	# the sole transform, applied mechanically, so there is no per-entry mapping
118	# and no drift. rbtd's lib.rs paths, the manifest role mirror, and the
119	# rbtdrk/rbtdrp .env consts all source these instead of hand-copying.
120	rbcc_emit_consts() {
121	  printf '%s\n' "// RBCC constants (rbcc_Constants.sh single-homed set)"
122	
123	  local z_name=""
124	  local z_stem=""
125	  local z_upper=""
126	  for z_name in \
127	    RBCC_moorings_dir    \
128	    RBCC_vessels_subdir  \
129	    RBCC_role_governor   \
130	    RBCC_role_retriever  \
131	    RBCC_role_director   \
132	    RBCC_role_payor      \
133	    RBCC_role_assay      \
134	    RBCC_role_mason      \
135	    RBCC_rbrr_file       \
136	    RBCC_rbrp_file       \
137	    RBCC_rbrm_file       \
138	    RBCC_rbrd_basename   \
139	    RBCC_rbrd_file       \
140	    RBCC_rbrs_file       \
141	    RBCC_rbrn_file       \
142	    RBCC_rbra_file       \
143	    RBCC_rbro_file       \
144	  ; do
145	    z_stem="${z_name#RBCC_}"
146	    z_upper="$(printf '%s' "${z_stem}" | tr '[:lower:]' '[:upper:]')"
147	    buz_emit_const "RBTDGC_${z_upper}" "${!z_name}" \
148	      || buc_die "rbcc_emit_consts: emit failed for ${z_name}"
149	  done
150	}
151	
152	######################################################################
153	# Internal Functions (zrbcc_*)
154	
155	zrbcc_kindle() {
156	  test -z "${ZRBCC_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbcc already kindled"
157	
158	  # Curl timeout bounds — all actionable curl sites use these
159	  readonly RBCC_CURL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT_SEC=10
160	  readonly RBCC_CURL_MAX_TIME_SEC=60
161	
162	  readonly ZRBCC_KINDLED=1
163	}
164	
165	zrbcc_sentinel() {
166	  test "${ZRBCC_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbcc not kindled - call zrbcc_kindle first"
167	}

# string consts to stdout, one `pub const` line per name/value pair via the
# shared buz_emit_const primitive (BUK must be kindled). The single-homed set:
# moorings/vessels dirs, credential roles, .env filenames. Each Rust const is
# RBTDGC_ + the RBCC stem (RBCC_ prefix stripped) uppercased; the value is
# carried verbatim. Bash stays mixed-case (RBCC_moorings_dir); the generated
# Rust is SCREAMING (RBTDGC_MOORINGS_DIR) per Rust convention — that casing is
# the sole transform, applied mechanically, so there is no per-entry mapping
# and no drift. rbtd's lib.rs paths, the manifest role mirror, and the
# rbtdrk/rbtdrp .env consts all source these instead of hand-copying.
rbcc_emit_consts() {
  printf '%s\n' "// RBCC constants (rbcc_Constants.sh single-homed set)"

  local z_name=""
  local z_stem=""
  local z_upper=""
  for z_name in \
    RBCC_moorings_dir    \
    RBCC_vessels_subdir  \
    RBCC_role_governor   \
    RBCC_role_retriever  \
    RBCC_role_director   \
    RBCC_role_payor      \
    RBCC_role_assay      \
    RBCC_role_mason      \
    RBCC_rbrr_file       \
    RBCC_rbrp_file       \
    RBCC_rbrm_file       \
    RBCC_rbrd_basename   \
    RBCC_rbrd_file       \
    RBCC_rbrs_file       \
    RBCC_rbrn_file       \
    RBCC_rbra_file       \
    RBCC_rbro_file       \
  ; do
    z_stem="${z_name#RBCC_}"
    z_upper="$(printf '%s' "${z_stem}" | tr '[:lower:]' '[:upper:]')"
    buz_emit_const "RBTDGC_${z_upper}" "${!z_name}" \
      || buc_die "rbcc_emit_consts: emit failed for ${z_name}"
  done
}

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (zrbcc_*)

zrbcc_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBCC_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rbcc already kindled"

  # Curl timeout bounds — all actionable curl sites use these
  readonly RBCC_CURL_CONNECT_TIMEOUT_SEC=10
  readonly RBCC_CURL_MAX_TIME_SEC=60

  readonly ZRBCC_KINDLED=1
}

zrbcc_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBCC_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbcc not kindled - call zrbcc_kindle first"
}

50	  ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
51	  ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
52	  # Gauntlet — release-qualification ladder, walks marshal-zero state through
53	  # canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. Pristine-lifecycle
54	  # case 1 is the entry-contract gate; preceding enrollment-validation runs
55	  # state-indifferent and is harmless on broken state. Fail-fast across
56	  # fixtures is provided by the for-loop's set -e.
57	  ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET=(
58	    "enrollment-validation"
59	    "pristine-lifecycle"
60	    "canonical-establish"
61	    "onboarding-sequence"
62	    "regime-validation"
63	    "regime-smoke"
64	    "dockerfile-hygiene"
65	    "hallmark-lifecycle"
66	    "tadmor"
67	    "moriah"
68	    "srjcl"
69	    "pluml"
70	  )
71	  # Skirmish — the "mini gauntlet": the depot->build->crucible chain WITHOUT
72	  # project-ID churn. canonical-invest reuses a standing operator-levied depot
73	  # (no levy, no unmake) where the gauntlet's pristine-lifecycle/canonical
74	  # -establish each levy a fresh project; pristine-lifecycle is dropped
75	  # entirely. onboarding-sequence then builds the crucible images (local kludge
76	  # + cloud ordain into the standing depot) and the four crucibles charge+run.
77	  # OPERATOR PRECONDITION: a canonical depot already levied — install canonical
78	  # prefixes and run rbw-dL by hand before this suite. Spends cloud build/GAR
79	  # but creates no GCP project per run.
80	  ZRBTE_SUITE_SKIRMISH=(
81	    "enrollment-validation"
82	    "canonical-invest"
83	    "onboarding-sequence"
84	    "regime-validation"
85	    "regime-smoke"
86	    "dockerfile-hygiene"
87	    "tadmor"
88	    "moriah"
89	    "srjcl"
90	    "pluml"
91	  )
92	  # Dogfight — standing-depot cloud-build viability probe. Sibling to skirmish
93	  # in the operator-precondition family (reuses a hand-levied depot, no levy,
94	  # no unmake) but charges NO crucible: it proves only the cloud-build → summon
95	  # → run path yields a runnable artifact. canonical-invest leads exactly as it
96	  # does in skirmish — re-mantling the governor and divest/re-investing
97	  # retriever + director so the dogfight fixture finds fresh credentials; the
98	  # dogfight fixture itself stays crucible-free. OPERATOR PRECONDITION: a
99	  # canonical depot already levied (the standing-depot setup skirmish assumes).
100	  ZRBTE_SUITE_DOGFIGHT=(
101	    "canonical-invest"
102	    "dogfight"
103	  )
104	  # Tadmor self-contained — fully local, no GCP/depot/project. Two fixtures in
105	  # sequence: kludge-tadmor builds BOTH vessels (sentry + bottle) locally and
106	  # commits each hallmark (the fixture owns the notch — same precedent as
107	  # onboarding's rbtdro_kludge_nameplate); then the tadmor crucible fixture
108	  # charges against the now-clean nameplate, runs the security cases, quenches.
109	  # The build is a separate fixture (nameplate passed explicitly) rather than a
110	  # self-charging tadmor fixture, because the crucible security cases resolve
111	  # their nameplate from the fixture name and would collide on "tadmor".
112	  ZRBTE_SUITE_TADMOR=(
113	    "kludge-tadmor"
114	    "tadmor"
115	  )
116	
117	  readonly ZRBTE_KINDLED=1
118	}

125	}

131	# Write-on-change, so unchanged files keep their mtime and cargo skips rebuilds.
132	# The build is the sole producer — there is no standalone generate command.
133	zrbte_codegen() {
134	  zrbte_sentinel
135

139	    || buc_die "Failed to generate colophon consts"
140	}

143	  zrbte_sentinel
144	
145	  zrbte_codegen

147	  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
148	  test -x "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" || buc_die "Theurge binary not found: ${ZRBTE_BINARY}"
149	}

154	    fast)     echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[*]}" ;;
155	    service)  echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE[*]}" ;;
156	    crucible) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE[*]}" ;;
157	    complete) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE[*]}" ;;
158	    gauntlet) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET[*]}" ;;
159	    skirmish) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SKIRMISH[*]}" ;;
160	    dogfight) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_DOGFIGHT[*]}" ;;
161	    tadmor)   echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_TADMOR[*]}" ;;

  ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE=("${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[@]}" "access-probe" "hallmark-lifecycle" "batch-vouch" "tadmor" "srjcl" "pluml")
  # Gauntlet — release-qualification ladder, walks marshal-zero state through
  # canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. Pristine-lifecycle
  # case 1 is the entry-contract gate; preceding enrollment-validation runs
  # state-indifferent and is harmless on broken state. Fail-fast across
  # fixtures is provided by the for-loop's set -e.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "pristine-lifecycle"
    "canonical-establish"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
    "hallmark-lifecycle"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )
  # Skirmish — the "mini gauntlet": the depot->build->crucible chain WITHOUT
  # project-ID churn. canonical-invest reuses a standing operator-levied depot
  # (no levy, no unmake) where the gauntlet's pristine-lifecycle/canonical
  # -establish each levy a fresh project; pristine-lifecycle is dropped
  # entirely. onboarding-sequence then builds the crucible images (local kludge
  # + cloud ordain into the standing depot) and the four crucibles charge+run.
  # OPERATOR PRECONDITION: a canonical depot already levied — install canonical
  # prefixes and run rbw-dL by hand before this suite. Spends cloud build/GAR
  # but creates no GCP project per run.
  ZRBTE_SUITE_SKIRMISH=(
    "enrollment-validation"
    "canonical-invest"
    "onboarding-sequence"
    "regime-validation"
    "regime-smoke"
    "dockerfile-hygiene"
    "tadmor"
    "moriah"
    "srjcl"
    "pluml"
  )
  # Dogfight — standing-depot cloud-build viability probe. Sibling to skirmish
  # in the operator-precondition family (reuses a hand-levied depot, no levy,
  # no unmake) but charges NO crucible: it proves only the cloud-build → summon
  # → run path yields a runnable artifact. canonical-invest leads exactly as it
  # does in skirmish — re-mantling the governor and divest/re-investing
  # retriever + director so the dogfight fixture finds fresh credentials; the
  # dogfight fixture itself stays crucible-free. OPERATOR PRECONDITION: a
  # canonical depot already levied (the standing-depot setup skirmish assumes).
  ZRBTE_SUITE_DOGFIGHT=(
    "canonical-invest"
    "dogfight"
  )
  # Tadmor self-contained — fully local, no GCP/depot/project. Two fixtures in
  # sequence: kludge-tadmor builds BOTH vessels (sentry + bottle) locally and
  # commits each hallmark (the fixture owns the notch — same precedent as
  # onboarding's rbtdro_kludge_nameplate); then the tadmor crucible fixture
  # charges against the now-clean nameplate, runs the security cases, quenches.
  # The build is a separate fixture (nameplate passed explicitly) rather than a
  # self-charging tadmor fixture, because the crucible security cases resolve
  # their nameplate from the fixture name and would collide on "tadmor".
  ZRBTE_SUITE_TADMOR=(
    "kludge-tadmor"
    "tadmor"
  )

  readonly ZRBTE_KINDLED=1
}

######################################################################
# Sentinel

zrbte_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBTE_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rbte not kindled - call zrbte_kindle first"
}

######################################################################
# Internal helpers

# zrbte_codegen() - Refresh all zipper-derived artifacts before compiling.
# Write-on-change, so unchanged files keep their mtime and cargo skips rebuilds.
# The build is the sole producer — there is no standalone generate command.
zrbte_codegen() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  rbz_generate_context "${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}" "${RBCC_tabtarget_context_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to generate tabtarget context"
  rbz_generate_consts "${RBCC_rbtdgc_consts_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to generate colophon consts"
}

zrbte_build_binary() {
  zrbte_sentinel

  zrbte_codegen
  buc_step "Building theurge"
  cargo build --manifest-path "${RBTE_MANIFEST}" || buc_die "cargo build failed"
  test -x "${ZRBTE_BINARY}" || buc_die "Theurge binary not found: ${ZRBTE_BINARY}"
}

zrbte_resolve_suite() {
  local z_suite="$1"
  case "${z_suite}" in
    fast)     echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_FAST[*]}" ;;
    service)  echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SERVICE[*]}" ;;
    crucible) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_CRUCIBLE[*]}" ;;
    complete) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_COMPLETE[*]}" ;;
    gauntlet) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_GAUNTLET[*]}" ;;
    skirmish) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_SKIRMISH[*]}" ;;
    dogfight) echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_DOGFIGHT[*]}" ;;
    tadmor)   echo "${ZRBTE_SUITE_TADMOR[*]}" ;;
    *)        buc_die "Unknown suite: ${z_suite} (expected fast|service|crucible|complete|gauntlet|skirmish|dogfight|tadmor)" ;;
  esac
}

######################################################################
# Public functions

# Rust const projection (colophons → RBTDGC_)

# rbz_emit_consts() - Emit the complete generated RBTDGC_ consts file to stdout.
# The single producer of rbtdgc_consts.rs content, shared by the theurge build
# (rbz_generate_consts) and rbq's freshness gate so the two never diverge.
# Two sections under one banner: the colophon group (this module binds the RBK
# projection prefixes — const name is RBTDGC_ + the enroll varname with its
# RBZ_ prefix stripped) followed by the rbcc single-homed set. rbcc_emit_consts
# is reachable here because every caller (rbq, the build, rblm) sources rbcc
# alongside rbz.
rbz_emit_consts() {
  zrbz_sentinel
  printf '%s\n' "// Generated by the theurge build — rbz colophons + rbcc constants. Do not edit."
  printf '%s\n' "// Regenerate: tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh"
  printf '%s\n' ""
  buz_emit_colophon_consts "RBTDGC_" "RBZ_" || buc_die "rbz_emit_consts: colophon emit failed"
  printf '%s\n' ""
  rbcc_emit_consts || buc_die "rbz_emit_consts: rbcc emit failed"
}

# rbz_generate_consts() - Write the colophon const file, only on difference.
# Args: target_path
# Emits to a temp file, diffs against the target, and copies only when changed
# (stable timestamps). The single producer for the generated file — rblm and
# the theurge build wrapper both call this; rbq's gate verifies via rbz_emit_consts.

Mounted **₣BP** (rbk-13-mvp-redundancy-cleanup), next pace **₢BPAAG** (fixture-codegen-projection).

## Pace goal

Generate the rbtd fixture-name consts from their bash author via the shared `buz_emit_const` primitive into the `RBTDGC_` file, and migrate consumers off the hand-mirror in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — mirroring the colophon (₢BPAAD) and RBCC (₢BPAAE) projections already landed.

## Heat context

Grab-bag *redundancy cleanup*: kill any constant hand-maintained on both sides of the bash↔Rust boundary. Locked rule — **single canonical bash author, or out of projection scope.** Five co-maintained groups feed one generated file (`RBTDGC_`, fed per-pair via the shared primitive). Colophons and the RBCC set are already projected; fixtures are this pace.

Recent: ₢BPAAE projected the RBCC single-homed set; `rbz_emit_consts` is now the single combiner (banner + colophon section + RBCC section), shared by the build and rbq's freshness gate.

## What I found — a premise correction

The docket says *"Fixture names are canonically authored in `rbte_engine.sh`... Single home exists — projectable without a tidy step."* Verifying against the code, that's **partially true**:

- **18 fixtures** appear in `rbte_engine.sh`'s 8 suite arrays (`ZRBTE_SUITE_*`). These map **1:1** to the 18 non-calibrant `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` consts. Genuinely bash-authored → projectable.
- **4 calibrant fixtures** (`calibrant-verdicts/fail-fast/progressing/sentinel`) have **no bash home** — `grep calibrant Tools/rbk/**/*.sh` is empty; they're Rust-only synthetic fixtures owned by `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs`. Per the locked rule (single bash author *or out*), these are **out of projection scope** — not a redundancy, correctly single-homed in Rust. They stay as hand-written manifest consts.
- There is **no single canonical fixture array** in bash today — names live spread across the 8 suite arrays (with duplicates). Emitting needs a declared list to loop, so a minimal tidy *is* required, contra the docket's "no tidy step."

Net effect on "migrate consumers off the hand-mirror": the hand-mirror doesn't fully retire — the 4 calibrant consts legitimately remain.

## Proposed approach

1. Declare the canonical 18-name fixture list + `rbte_emit_consts()` in `rbte_engine.sh` (mirrors `rbcc_emit_consts`); transform = value hyphen→underscore, uppercase → `RBTDGC_ENROLLMENT_VALIDATION` etc.
2. Wire a third section into the `rbz_emit_consts` combiner (confirm rbte is sourced in build / rbq-gate / rblm paths).
3. Repoint the 18 `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_*` consumers to `RBTDGC_*`; delete those 18 manifest consts; **keep** the 4 calibrant consts.
4. Verify: `rbw-tb` (deny-warnings), `rbw-tt`, `rbw-ts.fast`, `rbw-tq`.

Sequential, single-author work — opus-tier judgment on the bash-source shape, but mechanically constrained by the established primitive.

One decision worth your call before I start:

content.text:
A **calibrant** is a synthetic, self-contained test fixture whose verdicts are *known in advance* — it exists to test the theurge **engine itself**, not the Recipe Bottle system the engine normally exercises.

The metaphor is from instrumentation: a calibrant is a reference standard of known value you run through an instrument to confirm the instrument reads correctly. Here, the "instrument" is the theurge test engine, and the calibrant fixtures feed it cases with predetermined outcomes (deterministic Pass/Fail, fail-fast triggers, state-progression dispatch) so a bash blackbox testbench can assert the engine reports exit codes, stderr format, and fail-fast semantics correctly.

From `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs:17-34`, the four:

| Fixture | Cases | What it calibrates |
|---------|-------|--------------------|
| `calibrant-verdicts` | 4 | verdict-path coverage |
| `calibrant-fail-fast` | 3 | fail-fast halts subsequent cases |
| `calibrant-progressing` | 2 | probe Ok/Err dispatch (state-progressing) |
| `calibrant-sentinel` | 1 | suite-level fail-fast pivot |

Two properties that matter for the projection question we were just discussing:

- **They never shell out to bash** — all four declare empty `rbtdrm_required_colophons`, so they have no bash tabtarget coupling. They're "internal framework-test plumbing; not end-user-facing" (line 19).
- **They're Rust-authored** — defined entirely in `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs`, registered through the manifest, dispatched via `rbtdrc_crucible.rs`. There's no bash home, which is exactly why they fall *out* of the bash→Rust projection scope: nothing to project *from*.

So a calibrant tests the tester; the other 18 fixtures test Recipe Bottle.
I'll reslate ₢BPAAG via the gazette. Writing the corrected docket now.

# jjezs_reslate ₢BPAAG fixture-codegen-projection

## Character

Mechanical projection over a bash-authored group; mirrors the colophon/rbcc shape against the fixture-name owner. Carries a premise correction from the prior docket.

## Goal

Project the bash-authored fixture names into generated `RBTDGC_*` consts via the shared `buz_emit_const` primitive, and repoint their consumers off the hand-mirror in `rbtdrm_manifest.rs`.

## Shape

- Fixture names live spread across the `ZRBTE_SUITE_*` arrays in `rbte_engine.sh` — there is no single canonical array today. A minimal tidy is required: declare the fixture set once as a flat emit list; the suite arrays stay literal subsets (they select fixtures, they do not declare them). Suite-vs-list drift is already caught — an unknown fixture name fails resolution.
- Only the bash-authored fixtures project (those named in the suite arrays). The calibrant fixtures are Rust-only synthetic (`rbtdrl_calibrant.rs`, no bash home) and stay out of scope per the heat's single-bash-author rule — their `RBTDRM_FIXTURE_CALIBRANT_*` consts remain hand-written in the manifest. The hand-mirror shrinks; it does not vanish.
- Feed the names through the shared primitive into the `RBTDGC_` file (const name `RBTDGC_` + fixture stem, hyphens to underscores, uppercased); migrate the bash-authored manifest consumers and any inline fixture literals onto the generated consts. Mirror `rbcc_emit_consts` for the emit shape and `rbz_emit_consts` for the combiner wiring.
- Keep the runtime drift check alive until the diff-gate lands.

## Sources

- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_engine.sh` — fixture-name author (suite arrays)
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — fixture hand-mirror

content.text:
Traced it end to end. The slate lede **is** used, and it's load-bearing:

- `jjrz_parse_slate_input` (`jjrz_gazette.rs:265`) reads the Slate notice's lede as **silks**, validates it's non-empty, and returns `(silks, docket)`.
- The MCP dispatcher (`jjrm_mcp.rs:894`) hands that silks to `jjrsl_run_slate` as the `--silks` arg (`jjrsl_slate.rs:31`).
- `jjx_enroll`'s JSON params **deliberately omit** silks — the gazette lede is its *only* channel into the binary.

So removing it isn't a doc tweak: silks would have to relocate into the JSON params (the way `jjx_create` already carries `silks: p.silks` at `jjrm_mcp.rs:889`). That's a change across the MCP boundary — params struct, dispatcher, parser, and guidance.

And here's the thing — **removing the slate lede wouldn't fix the spook anyway.** Look at the three input parsers together:

| Notice | Lede | Validated |
|--------|------|-----------|
| `jjezs_slate` | silks | non-empty, kebab-case (no spaces) |
| `jjezs_reslate` | coronet | non-empty, 5 b64 chars |
| `jjezs_paddock` | firemark | non-empty |

All three are the *same shape*: one non-empty, whitespace-free token, then the body. My error — appending ` fixture-codegen-projection` after the coronet — would have failed equally on a slate notice, because silks can't contain spaces either. The spook wasn't "slate has a lede"; it was "I put two tokens where one belongs."

That actually points at a *simpler* fix than the one I proposed earlier. My earlier "slate=free-text vs reslate/paddock=bare identity" framing was itself slightly wrong — silks isn't free text. The accurate, uniform rule is:

> Every input notice's lede is **exactly one whitespace-free token** (silks / coronet / firemark) — nothing follows it on the `#` line. The body goes on the lines beneath.

One rule, all three slugs, and it's the rule I actually violated. That's both more correct and shorter than a per-slug table.

Want me to replace line 164 of `claude-jjk-core.md` with that single-rule statement (dropping my earlier per-slug proposal)? I'd leave the slate lede in place — it's load-bearing, and the spook is addressed by the uniform-token rule, not by removing it.

content.text:
Let me sharpen the asymmetry first, because I think it's not quite "slate vs reslate" — it's **value vs selector**:

- **slate's lede = a value being set** (silks — the new pace's name).
- **reslate's / paddock's lede = a selector** (the coronet / firemark of an *existing* entity to act on).

Same syntactic slot, opposite data-flow direction. That's the real wart. The lede is genuinely useful and essential for reslate and paddock — they *must* address an existing pace/heat, and the coronet is how. It's slate that's the odd one out: it has nothing to select (no coronet exists yet, firemark's in params), so the designers parked the one scalar slate needs — silks — in the lede slot. Removing the lede outright would break reslate/paddock; the thing to question is only slate's *repurposing* of it as a value.

On your "pace lede is immutable" guess — close, but: silks **is** mutable, via `jjx_relabel {coronet, silks}`. It's just not reachable through reslate. That's exactly the friction you're feeling: reslate rewrites the docket but can't touch the name, so a mis-slated name needs a *second*, different command.

**Why I'd steer away from moving silks into the body, though:** silks is a structured, validated field — kebab-case, used in commit messages, display headers, and relabel. Embedding it in freeform docket prose means inventing a convention (`## Silks:` line) and a parser to pull it back out and re-validate, and reslate's docket *diff* would start entangling name-changes with body-changes. That's more machinery, not less.

**The cleaner target is params, not body — and there's a strong consistency argument for it:** silks *already* travels via JSON params in both `jjx_create` and `jjx_relabel`. **Only slate routes it through the gazette lede.** So the consistent end-state is:

- silks → params on slate too (matching create/relabel) — gazette body becomes *only ever docket/content*, never identity or name;
- the lede becomes uniformly a **selector** (reslate=coronet, paddock=firemark; slate carries no lede);
- give reslate an optional `silks` param, so one call can rename + redocket — scratching your "fix issues" itch without a separate relabel.

The one wrinkle: **mass-reslate.** An optional `silks` *param* can't carry N distinct names for N notices, so bulk-rename would still need per-notice silks — which only fits in the gazette (and there, the body). But bulk-rename is rare (relabel is one-at-a-time today and nobody's complained), so I wouldn't let that rare case drag silks into the body for everyone. Single-pace rename-via-reslate covers the actual itch.

So my recommendation: **don't move it to body.** If the itch is just "fix a mis-slated name the same way I fix the docket," the proportionate change is an optional `silks` on reslate's params. If you want the deeper symmetry (lede = selector-only, silks→params everywhere), that's a clean cleanup but a real refactor across `jjrm_mcp.rs`, the parsers, and the guidance — worth a pace, not a same-session tweak.

Which itch are we actually scratching — just reslate-can-rename, or the full lede-symmetry cleanup?
